meta.title: SF-SPC-5: Security, Performance, and Data Sharing Fundamentals

questions.stem: Which TWO objects can be zero-copy cloned in the Snowflake platform? (Select TWO)

options.text: Tables (including their data)
Databases (creating a clone of the entire database with all its schemas and tables)
Virtual warehouses
Network policies

questions.explanation:
**A** and **B** are correct. Snowflake supports zero-copy cloning of tables, schemas, and databases. Cloning a database clones all its contained schemas and tables in a single metadata operation.

**C** is wrong because virtual warehouses are compute resources and cannot be cloned — they are created independently.

**D** is wrong because network policies are account-level configuration objects and do not support cloning.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/object-clone

questions.stem: Which object types can a the Snowflake platform data provider add to a share? (Choose the BEST answer)

options.text: Only tables
Tables, secure views, and secure UDFs
Tables, regular views, and stored procedures
Only secure views (tables cannot be shared directly)

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a Snowflake share can include tables, secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs. Secure views are often used to share a subset of a table or join result while hiding the underlying table definition.

**A** is wrong because secure views and secure UDFs can also be added to shares.

**C** is wrong because regular (non-secure) views cannot be added to shares — only secure views — and stored procedures cannot be shared directly.

**D** is wrong because tables can be shared directly.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/views-secure

questions.stem: Which the Snowflake platform tool allows a developer to visually inspect the execution plan of a completed query, including time spent in each operator?

options.text: EXPLAIN USING TEXT
Query Profile in Snowsight
SHOW QUERIES
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Query Profile in Snowsight (Snowflake's web UI) provides a visual, interactive view of a completed query's execution plan. It shows each operator node (TableScan, HashJoin, Aggregate, etc.), the time percentage spent in each, bytes processed, rows produced, and spilling information.

**A** is wrong because EXPLAIN USING TEXT shows a textual query plan before execution — it does not show runtime metrics.

**C** is wrong because SHOW QUERIES lists queries but does not provide execution plan details.

**D** is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY provides query metadata (duration, bytes scanned) but not the visual operator-level breakdown.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ui-query-profile

questions.stem: Which the Snowflake platform data type is used to store JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML data in a single column?

options.text: OBJECT
ARRAY
VARIANT
JSON

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the VARIANT data type is Snowflake's universal semi-structured data type. It can store any valid semi-structured value including JSON objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, and null — as well as data loaded from Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML files.

**A** is wrong because OBJECT is a Snowflake type for key-value maps, which is a subset of what VARIANT can hold.

**B** is wrong because ARRAY is a Snowflake type for ordered arrays, also a subset of VARIANT.

**D** is wrong because JSON is not a Snowflake column data type — VARIANT is used to store JSON data.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: In the Snowflake platform's RBAC model, which statement is TRUE about privilege inheritance?

options.text: Privileges are inherited from child roles to parent roles
Privileges are inherited from parent roles to child roles
Each role must be explicitly granted every privilege; inheritance does not apply
Privileges are inherited only if the INHERIT_PRIVILEGES flag is set on the role

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because in Snowflake's RBAC role hierarchy, when a child role is granted to a parent role, the parent role inherits all privileges held by the child role. A user who assumes the parent role therefore has access to all privileges granted to any role below it in the hierarchy.

**A** is wrong because stating that privileges flow from child to parent is describing the grant direction, not the inheritance model. The question asks about how inheritance works: a parent role accumulates and inherits privileges from child roles granted to it.

**C** is wrong because privilege inheritance is a core and automatic feature of Snowflake RBAC — explicit re-granting is not required.

**D** is wrong because INHERIT_PRIVILEGES is not a flag or parameter in Snowflake's RBAC model.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A virtual warehouse is configured with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 3 and MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 using the Standard scaling policy. The warehouse currently has 2 active clusters. The load drops significantly and there is no queuing. What happens?

options.text: The warehouse immediately scales down to 1 cluster.
The warehouse scales down to 0 clusters, suspending entirely.
The warehouse waits for 2-3 consecutive checks showing low load before scaling down a cluster.
The warehouse scales down only when manually resized by an administrator.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Snowflake uses a conservative scale-down approach for multi-cluster warehouses. Even with the Standard policy, Snowflake performs consecutive load checks and only removes a cluster after confirming that reduced capacity is sustained — typically after 2-3 consecutive 1-minute checks show the cluster is underutilized. This prevents thrashing from brief load dips. 

**A** is wrong because scale-down is not instantaneous. 

**B** is wrong because scale-down is automatic for multi-cluster warehouses. 

**D** is wrong because MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 prevents the warehouse from scaling below 1 cluster.

questions.stem: A table is set to DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0. What is the effect on Time Travel and Fail-Safe?

options.text: Time Travel is disabled (0 days); Fail-Safe still applies for 7 days after each DML operation
Time Travel is disabled; Fail-Safe is also disabled because the retention is 0
Only the ACCOUNTADMIN can query historic data because retention is 0
The table automatically converts to a transient table with no Time Travel or Fail-Safe

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on a permanent table disables Time Travel (you cannot query historical data), but Fail-Safe still applies. Fail-Safe is independent of the Time Travel setting and always provides 7 days of protection for permanent tables after each data change.

**B** is wrong because Fail-Safe is not disabled by setting retention to 0 on a permanent table.

**C** is wrong because no user (including ACCOUNTADMIN) can query historic data via SQL when retention is 0 — that is the Fail-Safe window only accessible via Snowflake Support.

**D** is wrong because the table remains a permanent table; it is not automatically converted to transient.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform administrator needs to track and limit the number of compute credits used by a specific virtual warehouse. Which Snowflake object should they create?

options.text: Network policy
Task
Row access policy
Resource monitor

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because a resource monitor allows administrators to define credit quotas and trigger actions (notify, suspend, suspend immediately) when a warehouse reaches defined thresholds. It can be applied at the account or individual warehouse level.

**A** is wrong because network policies control IP-based access, not credit consumption.

**B** is wrong because tasks are used to schedule SQL statements, not to limit warehouse credit consumption.

**C** is wrong because row access policies control data visibility in tables, not compute resource usage.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A Snowpipe is configured with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE. How are files ingested with this configuration?

options.text: Files are never loaded; AUTO_INGEST = FALSE disables the pipe.
Files are ingested on a fixed schedule defined in the pipe definition.
Files are ingested by calling the Snowpipe REST API endpoint insertFiles to submit file paths for loading.
Files are ingested automatically when they are uploaded to the internal stage associated with the pipe.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because when AUTO_INGEST = FALSE, Snowpipe does not listen for cloud event notifications. Instead, an external application or orchestration tool must call the Snowpipe REST API (specifically the insertFiles endpoint) with the list of file paths to be loaded. This is the REST API mode of Snowpipe.

**A** is wrong because the pipe is fully functional with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE — it just requires an API call to trigger ingestion.

**B** is wrong because Snowpipe does not have a built-in schedule; scheduling is done externally.

**D** is wrong because Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE does not monitor stage uploads — that behavior requires AUTO_INGEST = TRUE with cloud event notifications.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-snowpipe-intro

questions.stem: When examining a query profile in the Snowflake platform, a developer sees that the `TableScan` node shows `Bytes scanned: 500 GB` but `Bytes spilled to local storage: 200 GB`. What does 'bytes spilled to local storage' indicate?

options.text: 200 GB of query results were written to an internal Snowflake table as overflow.
The virtual warehouse ran out of in-memory space during query execution and wrote intermediate data to local SSD, degrading performance.
Snowflake moved 200 GB of data to the result cache for future query reuse.
The query read 200 GB of redundant data that was pruned after the initial scan.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because 'spilled to local storage' in the Snowflake query profile means that the warehouse's in-memory capacity was insufficient to hold all intermediate query data (e.g., hash tables for joins or sort buffers), causing Snowflake to spill overflow to the warehouse nodes' local SSD. This is significantly slower than in-memory operations and indicates the warehouse may be undersized for the query's memory requirements.

**A** is wrong because spilling does not write to user tables.

**C** is wrong because spilling is not related to the result cache.

**D** is wrong because pruned data is not scanned at all — spilling refers to intermediate data written mid-execution.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/querying-persisted-results

questions.stem: A table has a VARIANT column `payload` containing nested JSON. A Snowflake developer writes: `SELECT f.key, f.value FROM my_table, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => payload, RECURSIVE => TRUE) f`. What does the RECURSIVE => TRUE parameter do?

options.text: It causes FLATTEN to follow foreign key references to other tables
It causes FLATTEN to traverse all levels of nested JSON objects and arrays, not just the top level
It causes FLATTEN to run the operation multiple times until no new rows are produced
RECURSIVE is not a valid parameter for the FLATTEN function

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the RECURSIVE => TRUE option in FLATTEN causes it to recursively traverse nested structures. Without RECURSIVE, FLATTEN only expands the top-level array or object. With RECURSIVE => TRUE, it drills into nested objects and arrays at all depths, producing a row for each leaf value in the JSON tree.

**A** is wrong because FLATTEN only operates on the VARIANT data within the current row, not foreign key relationships.

**C** is wrong because RECURSIVE controls depth traversal, not repetition.

**D** is wrong because RECURSIVE is indeed a valid parameter for the FLATTEN function.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following statements are TRUE about the Snowflake platform's Column-Level Security using Dynamic Data Masking? (Select TWO)

options.text: A masking policy can be applied to columns of any data type, including VARIANT.
Masking policies are evaluated before row access policies when both are applied to the same table.
Only one masking policy can be assigned to a column at a time.
The masking policy function must return the same data type as the column it masks.

questions.explanation:
**C** and **D** are correct. Each column can have at most one masking policy applied at a time. The return data type of the masking policy function must match the data type of the masked column — for example, a policy on a VARCHAR column must return VARCHAR. 

**A** is wrong because masking policies support specific data types, and VARIANT columns are not directly supported by standard masking policies (a separate approach is needed for semi-structured data). 

**B** is wrong because row access policies are evaluated first, then masking policies are applied to the resulting rows — the order is row access first, then column masking.

questions.stem: A table column `event_data` is of type VARIANT and contains the following JSON: `{"user": {"id": 123, "name": "Alice"}, "action": "login"}`. Which query correctly extracts the user's name as a string?

options.text: SELECT event_data.user.name FROM events;
SELECT event_data['user']['name']::VARCHAR FROM events;
SELECT JSON_VALUE(event_data, '$.user.name') FROM events;
SELECT EXTRACT(user.name FROM event_data) FROM events;

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake uses bracket notation (event_data['user']['name']) to traverse nested JSON within a VARIANT column, and the ::VARCHAR cast converts the VARIANT result to a string.

**A** is wrong because dot notation works for traversing JSON paths in Snowflake, but the result is still a VARIANT type — however, `event_data:user:name::VARCHAR` (colon notation) is the standard Snowflake syntax, while `event_data.user.name` is not valid for VARIANT columns.

**C** is wrong because JSON_VALUE() is not a native Snowflake SQL function — this is a SQL Server / ISO function.

**D** is wrong because EXTRACT with this syntax is not valid Snowflake SQL for VARIANT data.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes the Economy scaling policy for multi-cluster virtual warehouses?

options.text: Snowflake adds a new cluster as soon as any query is queued.
Snowflake adds a new cluster only when it estimates that the current cluster(s) will be busy for at least 6 minutes.
The Economy policy prevents Snowflake from ever removing clusters once they are added.
The Economy policy disables automatic scaling and requires manual cluster management.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Economy scaling policy is conservative — Snowflake waits until it predicts the queued load will keep an additional cluster busy for at least 6 minutes before provisioning it. This avoids spinning up clusters for brief spikes, trading some query latency for lower credit consumption.

**A** is wrong because immediately provisioning on any queued query is the Standard (not Economy) scaling policy behavior.

**C** is wrong because both policies remove clusters when demand drops.

**D** is wrong because both Standard and Economy are automatic scaling policies.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster

questions.stem: A data consumer has mounted a shared database from a provider. Which role in the CONSUMER account is required to CREATE DATABASE from a share?

options.text: SYSADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN
Any role with IMPORT SHARE privilege
SECURITYADMIN
The PUBLIC role

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because to mount a share as a database in the consumer account, the role must have the IMPORT SHARE account-level privilege. By default, ACCOUNTADMIN has this privilege and can grant it to other roles.

**A** is incorrect because SYSADMIN does not inherently have IMPORT SHARE privilege — it must be explicitly granted.

**C** is incorrect because SECURITYADMIN handles security policies and user management, not data share imports.

**D** is incorrect because PUBLIC has no import privileges.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: An engineer needs an auto-incrementing surrogate key when inserting rows into a table. Which Snowflake feature provides this capability?

options.text: A column with IDENTITY property (e.g., id INT IDENTITY(1,1))
A stream with METADATA$ROW_ID
A task that calls NEXTVAL on insert
A materialized view with ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY 1)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake supports the IDENTITY property on integer columns, which automatically generates sequential values for each inserted row. Sequences can also be used explicitly with `nextval`, but the IDENTITY column property is the most direct way to create an auto-incrementing surrogate key.

**B** is wrong because METADATA$ROW_ID is an internal Snowflake identifier in streams, not a user-accessible auto-increment key.

**C** is wrong because a task is not the mechanism for generating surrogate keys during inserts.

**D** is wrong because ROW_NUMBER() in a materialized view does not generate persistent surrogate keys for new rows.

[Sequences](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/querying-sequences)

questions.stem: The Query Acceleration Service (QAS) is enabled on a warehouse. Which type of query benefits MOST from QAS?

options.text: Short, highly selective point-lookup queries that read a single row.
Large scans with outlier partitions — queries where a small number of partitions contain disproportionately more data than average.
JOIN-heavy queries between large tables with no filter predicates.
Queries that are waiting in the concurrency queue due to high user load.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Query Acceleration Service specifically targets queries with 'outlier' micro-partitions — situations where a few partitions are much larger than average, causing the query to spend most of its time processing those few partitions. QAS offloads that outlier work to Snowflake-managed serverless compute, preventing the outlier partitions from bottlenecking the overall query. 

**A** is wrong because point lookups are already fast due to pruning and don't benefit from parallelism. 

**C** is wrong because QAS does not address concurrency queuing — multi-cluster warehouses handle that. 

**D** is wrong because full table joins without filters rarely have outlier partition patterns that QAS can exploit.

questions.stem: A COPY INTO statement completes but the `LOAD_HISTORY` view shows some files were not loaded. The engineer confirms the files exist in the stage. What is the MOST LIKELY reason?

options.text: The files were too large and were automatically skipped by Snowflake
The files were already loaded by a previous COPY INTO statement and Snowflake's load history prevents re-loading the same files
The stage has insufficient permissions to read the files
The files are compressed and COPY INTO does not support compressed files

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake tracks each file's load status in a 64-day metadata store per table. If a file has already been successfully loaded by a previous COPY INTO into the same table, Snowflake will skip it in subsequent COPY INTO runs (unless FORCE = TRUE is specified). This prevents accidental duplicate loading.

**A** is wrong because file size does not cause automatic skipping.

**C** is wrong because if the stage had insufficient permissions, the COPY would fail with an error, not silently skip.

**D** is wrong because Snowflake natively supports gzip, bzip2, and other compressed file formats.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform administrator wants to understand why a specific query was slow. Which Snowflake feature provides a visual breakdown of execution nodes, time spent at each step, and data flow within the query?

options.text: Query Profile in Snowsight
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
EXPLAIN PLAN output
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Query Profile in Snowsight (the Snowflake web UI) provides an interactive, visual execution graph showing each operation node in the query plan, the time each node took, bytes scanned, rows produced, and spill information. It is the primary tool for diagnosing slow queries. 

**B** is wrong because QUERY_HISTORY provides overall query metadata (total duration, credits, status) but not node-level execution detail. 

**C** is wrong because EXPLAIN PLAN shows the estimated execution plan before running but does not show actual runtime statistics. 

**D** is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES shows table metadata, not query execution details.

questions.stem: Which TWO features does the Snowflake platform's column-level security encompass? (Select TWO)

options.text: Dynamic Data Masking — masking sensitive column values based on the querying role
External Tokenization — replacing sensitive values with tokens managed by an external tokenization provider
Row Access Policies — filtering rows based on user attributes
Column Encryption at Rest — encrypting specific columns with customer-managed keys

questions.explanation:
**A** and **B** are correct. Snowflake's column-level security features include Dynamic Data Masking (which replaces or obfuscates column values based on role) and External Tokenization (which integrates with third-party tokenization solutions to replace sensitive values with tokens).

**C** is wrong because Row Access Policies are a row-level security feature, not column-level security.

**D** is wrong because Snowflake encrypts all data at rest by default using AES-256, and Tri-Secret Secure is the customer-managed key feature — but this is not categorized as column-level security.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following metadata values does the Snowflake platform store for each column within a micro-partition? (Select TWO)

options.text: Min and max values
Row-level checksums
Null value count
Column encryption keys

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct because Snowflake stores min/max values and null counts per column within each micro-partition. This metadata is used by the pruning engine to skip micro-partitions that cannot contain matching rows.

**B** is wrong because row-level checksums are not stored as per-column metadata in micro-partitions.

**D** is wrong because encryption keys are managed at the account/key hierarchy level, not stored inside micro-partition column metadata.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: A table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. The table is dropped. Two days after it is dropped, an administrator decides to restore it using UNDROP TABLE. How much Time Travel history is available on the restored table immediately after UNDROP?

options.text: 7 days of history from before the drop (the full retention period).
5 days of history, because 2 days of the retention period elapsed while the table was dropped.
No history; UNDROP restores the table structure only, not historical data.
7 days of history plus an additional 2 days because Fail-Safe extends the window while a table is dropped.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because when a table is dropped, Time Travel continues to count down against the retention period. The dropped table's retention period began at the time of the drop. Since 2 days have passed since the drop, only 5 days of Time Travel history remain (7 - 2 = 5 days).

**A** is wrong because the clock does not reset on UNDROP — time elapsed since the drop counts against the retention period.

**C** is wrong because UNDROP fully restores the table including its historical data; it is not a structure-only operation.

**D** is wrong because Fail-Safe begins only after the Time Travel window expires and is not user-accessible through SQL.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A company implements SAML 2.0 Single Sign-On (SSO) with the Snowflake platform. Which role in Snowflake is responsible for configuring the SAML identity provider settings at the account level?

options.text: SYSADMIN
SECURITYADMIN
ACCOUNTADMIN
PUBLIC

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because configuring SAML 2.0 SSO at the account level (setting `SAML_IDENTITY_PROVIDER` and `SSO_LOGIN_PAGE`) requires ACCOUNTADMIN privileges, as these are account-level parameters.

**A** is wrong because SYSADMIN manages database objects, not account-level authentication settings.

**B** is wrong because while SECURITYADMIN handles user/role management, configuring the SAML IDP connection at the account level requires ACCOUNTADMIN.

**D** is wrong because PUBLIC has no administrative privileges.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer uses `TRY_CAST(data:price::STRING AS NUMBER)` on a VARIANT column where some rows have `price` as a string like `"N/A"`. What is returned for those rows?

options.text: An error is raised for each row where the cast fails.
NULL is returned for rows where the cast to NUMBER fails.
0 is returned as a default numeric value for failed casts.
The original string value 'N/A' is returned unchanged.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because TRY_CAST is the safe casting function in Snowflake that returns NULL instead of raising an error when a type conversion fails. For rows where `data:price` is 'N/A' (which cannot be converted to a NUMBER), TRY_CAST returns NULL.

**A** is wrong because TRY_CAST specifically avoids raising errors — that is the purpose of the TRY_ prefix (use CAST or :: for error-raising behavior).

**C** is wrong because TRY_CAST returns NULL, not 0, on failure.

**D** is wrong because TRY_CAST always returns the target type or NULL — it never returns the original value in the original type.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer uses `SELECT PARSE_JSON('{"score": 95}'):score`. What does this expression return?

options.text: The integer 95 as a NUMBER data type.
The VARIANT value 95 (number within a VARIANT container).
The string '95' as a VARCHAR.
An error because colon notation requires a column reference, not an inline PARSE_JSON call.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because PARSE_JSON() parses a JSON string and returns a VARIANT. Applying :score (colon notation) to the VARIANT navigates to the 'score' key and returns the value as a VARIANT. The result is a VARIANT containing the numeric value 95, not a native NUMBER. To get a NUMBER, you would need to cast: `PARSE_JSON('{"score": 95}'):score::NUMBER`.

**A** is wrong because the result is VARIANT, not NUMBER, without an explicit cast.

**C** is wrong because no cast to STRING is applied.

**D** is wrong because colon notation is valid on any VARIANT expression, not just column references.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: An engineer has already loaded a file `data_2024.csv` from an S3 external stage into a Snowflake table. They run the same COPY INTO command the next day without any changes. What happens by default?

options.text: The file is loaded again, creating duplicate rows.
The file is skipped because Snowflake tracks loaded files and does not reload them by default.
The COPY INTO command fails with an error about a duplicate file.
The file is loaded only if the S3 file timestamp has changed.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake tracks which files have already been loaded via `LOAD_HISTORY` metadata. By default, COPY INTO skips files that have already been successfully loaded to prevent duplicate ingestion.

**A** is wrong because the default behavior is specifically designed to prevent duplicate loading.

**C** is wrong because COPY INTO does not fail with an error for previously loaded files — it simply skips them.

**D** is wrong because Snowflake uses the file metadata hash/path for deduplication, not just the S3 timestamp. The `FORCE = TRUE` option can override this behavior to reload files.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform developer uses `EXPLAIN USING JSON SELECT * FROM sales WHERE region = 'WEST' AND sale_date = '2024-01-15'`. The output shows `partitionsTotal: 5000` and `partitionsAssigned: 4950`. What does this indicate?

options.text: Only 50 partitions will be scanned; the remaining 4950 are pruned.
The query is extremely well-optimized and nearly all partitions are pruned.
4950 partitions will be scanned out of 5000; very little pruning is occurring.
The query cannot be further optimized because region and sale_date are incompatible filter columns.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because `partitionsAssigned` represents the number of micro-partitions that will actually be scanned (cannot be pruned). With 4950 of 5000 partitions assigned, only 50 are being pruned — this is very poor pruning efficiency, likely because the table is not well-clustered on region or sale_date. 

**A** is wrong because nearly all partitions being assigned (scanned) is the opposite of well-optimized pruning. 

**B** is wrong because partitionsAssigned = partitions TO BE SCANNED (not pruned). 

**D** is wrong because the EXPLAIN result indicates a clustering/pruning problem, not query syntax incompatibility.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about the Snowflake platform's schema detection for semi-structured data are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.text: INFER_SCHEMA can automatically detect column names and types from staged Parquet, ORC, Avro, and JSON files.
Schema detection using INFER_SCHEMA works only for CSV files.
GENERATE_COLUMN_LIST returns a column definition string from INFER_SCHEMA output that can be used in CREATE TABLE statements.
INFER_SCHEMA requires the files to be loaded into a Snowflake table before the schema can be detected.

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. INFER_SCHEMA is a Snowflake table function that reads staged files (Parquet, ORC, Avro, JSON with STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY) and returns the inferred column names and data types without loading the data into a table first. GENERATE_COLUMN_LIST is a helper function that formats the INFER_SCHEMA output into a usable column definition string for CREATE TABLE or COPY INTO statements.

**B** is wrong because INFER_SCHEMA does not work with CSV — CSV files do not carry embedded schema information.

**D** is wrong because INFER_SCHEMA reads files directly from the stage, before any table load.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/semistructured-intro

questions.stem: Which TWO objects can a data provider add to a the Snowflake platform SHARE? (Select TWO)

options.text: Secure views
Regular (non-secure) views
Tables
Virtual warehouses

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. A Snowflake SHARE can contain: tables, secure views, secure materialized views, secure UDFs, and external tables. The consumer can then query these objects via a shared database.

**B** is wrong because regular (non-secure) views cannot be added to a share — only secure views are permitted to prevent information leakage through query plan optimization.

**D** is wrong because virtual warehouses are compute resources owned by individual accounts and cannot be shared — the consumer uses their own warehouse (or the provider's warehouse for Reader Accounts) to query shared data.

[Data Sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)

questions.stem: A table's Time Travel retention period is set to 7 days. After 7 days, the data enters Fail-Safe. How long does Fail-Safe last, and who can recover data from it?

options.text: Fail-Safe lasts 90 days and can be queried using the AT(FAIL_SAFE => TRUE) syntax.
Fail-Safe lasts 7 days and can only be recovered by Snowflake Support — it is not accessible via SQL.
Fail-Safe lasts indefinitely and is automatically converted to a long-term backup archive.
Fail-Safe lasts 24 hours and can be recovered by the ACCOUNTADMIN role using UNDROP TABLE.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Fail-Safe provides an additional 7-day retention period after Time Travel expires, but it is exclusively managed by Snowflake Support. Users cannot access Fail-Safe data via SQL commands — it is a last-resort disaster recovery mechanism for catastrophic failures. 

**A** is wrong because Fail-Safe lasts 7 days (not 24 hours) and is not accessible via UNDROP TABLE. 

**C** is wrong because AT(FAIL_SAFE => TRUE) is not valid Snowflake syntax. 

**D** is wrong because Fail-Safe has a fixed 7-day window and is not converted to long-term archives.

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer has a stream `orders_stream` on the `orders` table. The `orders` table has a Time Travel retention period of 1 day. The stream has not been consumed for 3 days. What is the state of the stream?

options.text: The stream is paused and will resume automatically when consumed.
The stream only contains the last 24 hours of changes, discarding earlier ones.
The stream is stale and must be recreated to capture new changes.
The stream is stale but can be reset to the current timestamp using ALTER STREAM.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because a stream becomes stale when its offset falls behind the Time Travel retention period of the source table. If the stream was not consumed for 3 days but the table only has 1 day of Time Travel, the stream's offset position is now beyond the available Time Travel window. The stream is stale and the historical change data it was tracking is no longer accessible. The stream must be dropped and recreated to resume CDC.

**A** is incorrect because stale streams are not paused — they are in an invalid state that requires recreation.

**B** is incorrect because stale streams do not self-trim to the available Time Travel window.

**D** is incorrect because there is no ALTER STREAM command to reset a stale stream's offset.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform administrator checks `SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('orders', '(order_date)')` and receives an `average_depth` of 8. What does this indicate?

options.text: The table has 8 micro-partitions, which is optimal.
On average, 8 micro-partitions overlap for any given ORDER_DATE value, indicating poor clustering.
The clustering key spans 8 columns, which exceeds the recommended maximum.
8% of micro-partitions are fully pruned by the clustering key, which is suboptimal.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `average_depth` represents the average number of micro-partitions that overlap for any given value of the clustering key column(s). A lower depth means better clustering — a depth of 8 is high, meaning data for a given ORDER_DATE is spread across many micro-partitions and pruning will be less effective. A target depth is generally below 3 for actively queried tables.

**A** is wrong because average_depth refers to overlap depth, not the count of micro-partitions.

**C** is wrong because average_depth does not refer to the number of columns in the clustering key.

**D** is wrong because average_depth is not a percentage metric.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: How does the Snowflake platform's Fail-Safe period differ from Time Travel?

options.text: Fail-Safe data can be queried with SQL using AT or BEFORE clauses, unlike Time Travel
Fail-Safe replaces Time Travel for Standard edition accounts
Fail-Safe data is stored in a separate cloud region for disaster recovery
Fail-Safe is a 7-day period (for permanent tables) after Time Travel expires; it is managed by Snowflake and not user-accessible via SQL

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Fail-Safe is a 7-day emergency recovery window that begins after the Time Travel period expires for permanent tables. Unlike Time Travel, Fail-Safe data cannot be queried directly by users — it can only be accessed by Snowflake Support for disaster recovery purposes. It is not configurable and always lasts 7 days for permanent tables.

**A** is wrong because Fail-Safe is not accessible via SQL AT/BEFORE syntax.

**B** is wrong because Fail-Safe exists alongside Time Travel (even when Time Travel is 0 days), not as a replacement.

**C** is wrong because Fail-Safe data is not replicated to a separate region — it uses the same underlying storage.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform administrator creates a masking policy: `CREATE MASKING POLICY email_mask AS (val STRING) RETURNS STRING -> CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() IN ('ANALYST') THEN val ELSE '***MASKED***' END;`. The policy is applied to the `email` column in the `customers` table. A user using the PUBLIC role queries the table. What does the `email` column return?

options.text: The full email address, because PUBLIC is a parent of all roles.
'***MASKED***' because the user's current role is PUBLIC, not ANALYST.
NULL because masking policies return NULL for unauthorized roles.
An error because masking policies require the user to have ANALYST role granted.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the masking policy evaluates CURRENT_ROLE() at query time. The user is operating under the PUBLIC role, which is not in the ('ANALYST') list, so the ELSE branch returns '***MASKED***'.

**A** is wrong because the policy checks the CURRENT_ROLE(), not role hierarchy — the PUBLIC role being a parent does not satisfy the ANALYST check.

**C** is wrong because the masking policy explicitly returns '***MASKED***' (a string), not NULL, for non-ANALYST roles.

**D** is wrong because masking policies do not error on unauthorized roles — they return the masked value.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: An engineer needs to implement a CDC pipeline using Snowflake streams and tasks. Which TWO statements about streams are CORRECT? (Select TWO)

options.text: A stream becomes stale if its offset advances beyond the Time Travel retention period of the source table.
Consuming a stream in a DML transaction advances the stream offset only if the transaction commits successfully.
Multiple streams on the same table share the same offset position.
SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() returns TRUE even if no new rows have been inserted since the stream was last consumed.

questions.explanation:
**A** and **B** are correct. A stream becomes stale if its offset falls behind beyond the Time Travel retention period — after that, the captured change data is no longer available. When a stream is consumed inside a DML transaction (e.g., INSERT INTO ... SELECT FROM stream), the offset advances only if the transaction commits; if it rolls back, the stream offset remains unchanged.

**C** is wrong because each stream on a table maintains its own independent offset.

**D** is wrong because SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() returns FALSE when there is no new unconsumed data in the stream.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: A warehouse is resized from Medium to Large WHILE queries are actively running on it. What happens to the in-flight queries?

options.text: In-flight queries are immediately killed and must be restarted on the new Large warehouse.
The resize is rejected until all running queries complete.
In-flight queries continue running on the existing Medium-sized compute; the resize applies to subsequently queued or submitted queries.
The warehouse splits: existing queries finish on Medium nodes while new queries start on Large nodes simultaneously.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Snowflake applies warehouse resizes gracefully. Queries that are already running continue on the existing cluster configuration. The new size takes effect for subsequent queries. This means a resize mid-execution does not disrupt running work.

**A** is incorrect because Snowflake does not kill in-flight queries on resize.

**B** is incorrect because resizes are not blocked by active queries.

**D** is incorrect because the warehouse does not split into concurrent Medium and Large clusters during a resize.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: Which virtual warehouse size is the SMALLEST available in the Snowflake platform?

options.text: X-Small (XS)
Nano
Micro
Small (S)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because X-Small (XS) is the smallest virtual warehouse size in Snowflake, consuming 1 credit per hour. 

**B** is wrong because Small is the next size up (2 credits/hr). C and D are wrong because Micro and Nano are not valid Snowflake warehouse sizes.

questions.stem: A data analyst runs a query and receives results. The exact same query is run again 10 minutes later without any changes to the underlying table. What is the expected behavior?

options.text: The query is blocked until the previous result expires.
Snowflake returns the cached result instantly from the result cache, consuming no compute credits.
Snowflake uses the local disk cache on the warehouse nodes to speed up execution.
The warehouse re-executes the query, consuming compute credits.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake's result cache persists query results for 24 hours. If the identical query is re-executed within that window and the underlying data has not changed, Snowflake serves the result directly from the result cache without engaging the virtual warehouse, consuming zero compute credits. 

**A** is wrong because the result cache prevents re-execution. 

**C** is wrong because the local disk cache requires the warehouse to be active and does not eliminate compute use. 

**D** is wrong because Snowflake does not block repeat queries.

questions.stem: A company needs to run two separate workloads simultaneously: a heavy ETL process and interactive BI queries. Both workloads must not impact each other's performance. What is the BEST the Snowflake platform approach?

options.text: Schedule the ETL to run after business hours so it does not conflict with BI.
Use a single 4X-Large warehouse shared between both workloads.
Enable multi-cluster mode on a single warehouse to split the workloads.
Create two separate virtual warehouses — one for ETL and one for BI queries.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Snowflake's architecture allows multiple virtual warehouses to operate independently on the same underlying storage. Creating separate warehouses for ETL and BI provides complete workload isolation — each warehouse has dedicated compute resources and neither can starve the other. 

**A** is wrong because a shared warehouse means both workloads compete for the same concurrency slots and memory. 

**C** is wrong because multi-cluster is designed to scale one warehouse type for concurrent identical workloads, not to separate different workload types. 

**B** is wrong because scheduling avoidance is an operational workaround, not an architectural solution.

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform account on the Enterprise edition has Time Travel enabled with the maximum 90-day retention. Which TWO Time Travel query syntaxes are valid for accessing historical data? (Select TWO)

options.text: `SELECT * FROM orders AT(OFFSET => -86400);`
`SELECT * FROM orders HISTORY(DAYS => 1);`
`SELECT * FROM orders AT(STATEMENT => '8e5d0ca9-005e-44e6-b858-a8f5b37c5726');`
`SELECT * FROM orders TIMETRAVEL OFFSET -86400;`

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. The `AT(OFFSET => -N)` syntax returns data as of N seconds before the current time. The `AT(STATEMENT => 'query_id')` syntax returns data as of the time immediately after the specified query statement executed. Both are valid Time Travel syntaxes.

**B** is wrong because `HISTORY(DAYS => 1)` is not valid Snowflake SQL syntax.

**D** is wrong because `TIMETRAVEL OFFSET` is not valid Snowflake SQL syntax.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: An application developer creates a sequence and uses it in an INSERT statement: `INSERT INTO orders (id, product) SELECT orders_seq.NEXTVAL, product FROM staging`. What happens if two concurrent sessions insert rows simultaneously using the same sequence?

options.text: One session blocks until the other completes to ensure sequential values.
Sequences are session-scoped, so each session has its own independent counter starting from 1.
Snowflake guarantees that each session receives unique, non-overlapping sequence values.
Both sessions generate duplicate sequence values, causing primary key violations.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Snowflake sequences are designed for concurrent use — multiple sessions can call NEXTVAL simultaneously and each will receive unique values without collision. Snowflake guarantees uniqueness but not strict monotonic ordering across concurrent sessions (values may not be sequential, but they will be unique). 

**A** is wrong because sequences do not generate duplicate values across concurrent sessions. 

**B** is wrong because sequences do not cause blocking — concurrent access is one of their key design properties. 

**D** is wrong because sequences are account/schema-scoped objects, not session-scoped.

questions.stem: An engineer creates a serverless task using `USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE = 'MEDIUM'`. How is compute billed for this task?

options.text: A Medium virtual warehouse is created, billed at 4 credits/hr for the duration of the task.
Snowflake manages the compute using serverless resources billed per-second based on actual compute used, not warehouse uptime.
The task is free because serverless tasks do not consume credits.
The task must be assigned to an existing warehouse; USER_TASK_MANAGED is only a size hint.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because serverless tasks (using USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE instead of WAREHOUSE) use Snowflake-managed compute resources that spin up and down automatically. Billing is based on the actual cloud services compute consumed during task execution, not a full warehouse-hour allocation. The INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE is a hint for initial sizing that Snowflake may adjust based on historical runs.

**A** is wrong because serverless tasks do not create or bill for a traditional virtual warehouse.

**C** is wrong because serverless tasks do consume compute credits, just differently than warehouse-based billing.

**D** is wrong because USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE is the mechanism that opts the task into serverless mode, not a warehouse hint.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A Snowpipe is configured with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE and an SQS event notification on an S3 bucket. A new file arrives but Snowpipe fails to ingest it. Upon investigation, the engineer finds the file has a column type mismatch. What happens to the file?

options.text: Snowpipe retries the file indefinitely until the schema is fixed
The file is quarantined in a Snowflake-managed error stage automatically
The file remains in the S3 bucket; Snowpipe logs the error in SNOWPIPE_INGEST_HISTORY and the file stays in a 'failed' state unless FORCE is used
Snowpipe partial-loads the rows that pass validation and discards the failing rows with no notification

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because when a file fails to load due to data errors, Snowpipe logs the error in the load history and marks the file as failed. The file remains in the source stage (S3 bucket). The engineer can investigate via COPY_HISTORY and VALIDATE functions. Snowpipe does not automatically retry files that have data errors — they must be corrected and re-queued or loaded with FORCE = TRUE.

**A** is wrong because Snowpipe does not retry data-error files indefinitely.

**B** is wrong because Snowflake does not have an automatic 'error quarantine stage'.

**D** is wrong because the default ON_ERROR for Snowpipe is ABORT_STATEMENT — the entire file is rejected.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-snowpipe-intro

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about schema detection for semi-structured data in the Snowflake platform are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.text: INFER_SCHEMA can automatically detect column names and types from Parquet and JSON files in a stage
INFER_SCHEMA works on CSV files by reading the first row as a header
The USING TEMPLATE clause in CREATE TABLE AS SELECT can use the output of INFER_SCHEMA to create a typed table
Schema detection requires Business Critical edition because it accesses external storage

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. INFER_SCHEMA is a Snowflake table function that reads staged files (Parquet, JSON, Avro, ORC) and detects the column names and data types. The result can be used with CREATE TABLE ... USING TEMPLATE (SELECT ARRAY_AGG(OBJECT_CONSTRUCT(*)) FROM TABLE(INFER_SCHEMA(...))) to automatically create a typed table matching the file's schema.

**B** is wrong because INFER_SCHEMA works on semi-structured binary formats like Parquet; for CSV, PARSE_HEADER = TRUE is used in a file format, but INFER_SCHEMA is not the tool for CSV.

**D** is wrong because INFER_SCHEMA is available on all Snowflake editions.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/semistructured-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer creates a sequence and uses it in an INSERT statement: `INSERT INTO orders (id, product) SELECT orders_seq.NEXTVAL, product FROM staging`. What happens if two concurrent sessions insert rows simultaneously using the same sequence?

options.text: Both sessions generate duplicate sequence values, causing primary key violations.
Snowflake guarantees that each session receives unique, non-overlapping sequence values.
One session blocks until the other completes to ensure sequential values.
Sequences are session-scoped, so each session has its own independent counter starting from 1.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake sequences are designed for concurrent use — multiple sessions can call NEXTVAL simultaneously and each will receive unique values without collision. Snowflake guarantees uniqueness but not strict monotonic ordering across concurrent sessions (values may not be sequential, but they will be unique).

**A** is wrong because sequences do not generate duplicate values across concurrent sessions.

**C** is wrong because sequences do not cause blocking — concurrent access is one of their key design properties.

**D** is wrong because sequences are account/schema-scoped objects, not session-scoped.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tasks-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer queries a VARIANT column with `SELECT v:price::FLOAT FROM orders`. For some rows, the `price` key is missing. What does Snowflake return for those rows?

options.text: An error is raised because the path does not exist
0.0 is returned as the default FLOAT value
NULL is returned for rows where the key does not exist
The query skips rows where the key is missing

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because when a VARIANT path does not exist for a particular row, Snowflake returns SQL NULL rather than an error. The colon path syntax (`v:price`) gracefully returns NULL for missing keys, and casting NULL::FLOAT still yields NULL. This is a key advantage of Snowflake's schemaless VARIANT handling.

**A** is wrong because missing VARIANT paths do not raise errors.

**B** is wrong because Snowflake does not substitute default type values — it returns NULL.

**D** is wrong because the query processes all rows; rows with missing paths simply return NULL.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that after restarting a virtual warehouse and running their first query, it is slower than subsequent identical queries. What BEST explains this behavior?

options.text: The Cloud Services layer needs to re-authenticate the user after each warehouse restart.
The first query warms up the local disk cache on the warehouse nodes; subsequent queries benefit from cached micro-partition data.
The query result cache is cleared when a warehouse restarts, forcing re-computation.
The optimizer generates a less efficient plan for the first query after a restart.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the local disk (SSD) cache on warehouse nodes is empty after a restart. The first query must read micro-partitions from remote object storage (S3/Azure/GCS), which is slower. Subsequent identical queries can read from the populated SSD cache, which is much faster. This is called cache warming.

**A** is wrong because authentication is handled by the Cloud Services layer and is not affected by warehouse restarts.

**C** is wrong because the query result cache is in the Cloud Services layer and is not cleared when a warehouse restarts.

**D** is wrong because the optimizer uses the same plan — the difference is the data access path.

[Virtual Warehouses Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)

questions.stem: An analyst runs a query that aggregates 2 years of sales data but always filters on the most recent 30 days for reporting. The table has 500 million rows. The query consistently scans 100% of partitions. Which single action would MOST improve partition pruning for this use case?

options.text: Define a clustering key on the sales_date column so recent data colocates in fewer micro-partitions.
Enable the Search Optimization Service on the sales_date column.
Create a materialized view pre-aggregating the last 30 days of data.
Increase the warehouse size from Medium to X-Large.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because when queries consistently filter on a date column (sales_date) but data is not physically colocated by that column, every query scans all micro-partitions. Defining a clustering key on sales_date causes Snowflake to reorganize micro-partitions so that rows with the same date are colocated, enabling the pruning engine to skip the vast majority of micro-partitions for 30-day queries.

**B** is wrong because Search Optimization Service is designed for high-cardinality equality lookups (point lookups on IDs, etc.) — it is less effective for date-range queries on clustered data.

**C** is wrong because a materialized view would help query speed but does not improve pruning on the base table.

**D** is wrong because increasing warehouse size speeds up full scans but does not add pruning intelligence.

[Micro-partitions and Data Clustering](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: A company on the Standard edition asks which features are NOT available to them. Which TWO features are exclusive to Enterprise edition and above? (Select TWO)

options.text: Time Travel retention period of up to 90 days
Multi-cluster warehouses
Data sharing with reader accounts
Fail-Safe data recovery

questions.explanation:
**A** and **B** are correct. Time Travel retention of up to 90 days (Standard is capped at 1 day) and multi-cluster warehouses are both Enterprise edition features not available on Standard.

**C** is wrong because data sharing and reader accounts are available on Standard edition — data sharing is a core Snowflake feature not gated to Enterprise.

**D** is wrong because Fail-Safe (7-day recovery period) is available on all editions for standard tables.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer has a VARIANT column containing an array of strings: `["red","green","blue"]`. They want one row per color value. Which SQL pattern accomplishes this?

options.text: `SELECT f.value FROM my_table, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => my_table.colors) f;`
`SELECT UNNEST(colors) FROM my_table;`
`SELECT SPLIT(colors, ',') FROM my_table;`
`SELECT PARSE_JSON(colors) FROM my_table;`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because LATERAL FLATTEN is the Snowflake function for expanding arrays (and objects) in VARIANT columns into multiple rows. The LATERAL keyword allows FLATTEN to reference the current row's column. The output column `f.value` contains each array element as a separate row.

**B** is wrong because UNNEST is a PostgreSQL/BigQuery function that does not exist in Snowflake's SQL dialect.

**C** is wrong because SPLIT returns an array, not multiple rows, and the input is already a VARIANT, not a delimited string.

**D** is wrong because PARSE_JSON parses a string into VARIANT — the data is already in VARIANT form.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: An engineer resizes a virtual warehouse from X-Small to Small. How many credits per hour will the Small warehouse consume?

options.text: 2 credits per hour
1 credit per hour
4 credits per hour
8 credits per hour

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because each warehouse size doubles the credit consumption. X-Small consumes 1 credit/hr, Small consumes 2 credits/hr, Medium consumes 4 credits/hr, and so on.

**B** is wrong because 1 credit/hr is the X-Small rate, not the Small rate.

**C** is wrong because 4 credits/hr is the Medium rate.

**D** is wrong because 8 credits/hr is the Large rate.

[Virtual Warehouse Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)

questions.stem: An application developer creates a materialized view `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_daily_sales AS SELECT sale_date, SUM(amount) total FROM sales GROUP BY sale_date`. A new row is inserted into the `sales` table. How does the materialized view stay current?

options.text: Snowflake automatically and incrementally maintains the materialized view as DML changes the base table.
The materialized view must be manually refreshed using ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REFRESH.
The materialized view is invalidated and returns empty results until the next manual refresh.
The materialized view is rebuilt from scratch every hour on a background schedule.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake materialized views are automatically maintained by Snowflake's background services. When DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) change the base table, Snowflake incrementally updates the materialized view to reflect those changes. This maintenance is transparent to users and happens without manual intervention. 

**B** is wrong because manual refresh is not required — automatic maintenance is a core feature of Snowflake materialized views. 

**C** is wrong because maintenance is incremental and event-driven, not hourly full rebuilds. 

**D** is wrong because the materialized view is not invalidated by base table changes.

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. An analyst wants to see the data as it existed exactly 10 days ago. Which Time Travel syntax is correct?

options.text: SELECT * FROM orders BEFORE(DAYS => 10);
SELECT * FROM orders AT(OFFSET => -864000);
SELECT * FROM orders AS OF TIMESTAMP_AGO(10, 'DAY');
SELECT * FROM orders HISTORY(10 DAYS AGO);

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the AT(OFFSET => ...) syntax uses a relative offset in seconds from the current time. 10 days = 10 × 24 × 60 × 60 = 864,000 seconds, so `AT(OFFSET => -864000)` returns data from 10 days ago. Negative offset means going back in time.

**A** is wrong because `BEFORE(DAYS => 10)` is not valid Snowflake syntax — BEFORE accepts TIMESTAMP, OFFSET (in seconds), or STATEMENT.

**C** is wrong because TIMESTAMP_AGO() is not a Snowflake function.

**D** is wrong because HISTORY() is not a valid Snowflake Time Travel clause.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: A provider uses the Snowflake platform's Secure Data Sharing. The consumer account is in a different Snowflake region. The provider has NOT set up database replication. What happens when the consumer tries to access the shared database?

options.text: The consumer can access the shared database because Snowflake automatically routes queries across regions
The consumer cannot access the shared database; cross-region sharing requires the provider to replicate the database to the consumer's region
The consumer receives a read-only copy that is refreshed every 24 hours
The consumer can access the shared database but with a 15-minute latency for cross-region data transfer

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake's native data sharing works within the same cloud region. If the consumer is in a different region (even the same cloud provider), the provider must first replicate the database to a Snowflake account in the consumer's region and then share from that secondary. Without replication, the consumer cannot access the cross-region share.

**A** is wrong because Snowflake does not automatically route cross-region sharing queries.

**C** is wrong because Snowflake shares provide live access, not 24-hour refresh copies, and in any case the share is inaccessible without replication.

**D** is wrong because there is no 15-minute latency mechanism — cross-region access simply does not work without replication.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: Which cloud object storage service does the Snowflake platform use as its storage layer when deployed on AWS?

options.text: Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store)
Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)
Amazon EFS (Elastic File System)
Amazon Glacier

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake uses Amazon S3 as its underlying object storage on AWS. Data is stored as compressed, columnar micro-partitions in S3.

**A** is wrong because EBS is block storage attached to EC2 instances and is not used as Snowflake's data layer.

**C** is wrong because EFS is a file system service not used by Snowflake's storage layer.

**D** is wrong because Glacier is archival storage and not the active storage layer used by Snowflake.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: An engineer needs to load a CSV file from their local laptop into a the Snowflake platform internal stage. Which command uploads the file from local disk to the stage?

options.text: COPY INTO @my_stage FROM '/local/path/data.csv'
PUT file:///local/path/data.csv @my_stage
INSERT INTO @my_stage SELECT * FROM '/local/path/data.csv'
UPLOAD '/local/path/data.csv' TO @my_stage

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the PUT command is used to upload files from a local file system to a Snowflake internal stage. The syntax is `PUT file://<local_path> @<stage_name>`. PUT is executed from the SnowSQL CLI or Snowflake drivers. 

**A** is wrong because COPY INTO is used to load data from a stage into a table, not to upload files to a stage. 

**C** is wrong because UPLOAD is not a valid Snowflake command. 

**D** is wrong because INSERT INTO is for inserting row data, not file uploading.

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform administrator needs to allow a third-party ETL tool to connect using key-pair authentication. Which parameter is set on the Snowflake user object to register the public key?

options.text: ALTER USER etl_user SET PASSWORD = '<public_key>';`
`GRANT KEY PAIR TO USER etl_user USING '<public_key_value>';`
`ALTER USER etl_user SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key_value>';`
`ALTER USER etl_user SET API_KEY = '<public_key_value>';`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because key-pair authentication in Snowflake is configured by setting the `RSA_PUBLIC_KEY` parameter on the user object using `ALTER USER ... SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY`. The ETL tool then authenticates using the corresponding private key. 

**A** is wrong because PASSWORD is for password-based authentication, not key-pair; setting the public key as a password would not enable key-pair auth. 

**B** is wrong because API_KEY is not a valid Snowflake user parameter. 

**D** is wrong because GRANT KEY PAIR is not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: Which the Snowflake platform feature provides an immutable, tamper-evident log of all SQL operations, login events, and data access across an account?

options.text: The Snowflake Data Exchange audit trail
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
Snowflake Access History (ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY)

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY provides detailed records of all data access events — including which queries accessed which base table columns and rows, by which user, at what time. This view is designed for data governance, compliance auditing, and sensitivity classification tracking. 

**A** is wrong because QUERY_HISTORY shows query text and execution metadata but does not detail which specific columns or rows were accessed. 

**B** is wrong because the Data Exchange is a data sharing marketplace, not an audit log. 

**C** is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS shows column metadata definitions, not access audit logs.

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform masking policy is defined that shows full SSN values to users with the `PII_VIEWER` role and shows `'***-**-****'` to all others. The masking policy is attached to the `ssn` column of the `customers` table. A user with the `ANALYST` role creates a view `SELECT ssn FROM customers`. When a `PII_VIEWER` role user queries the view, what do they see?

options.text: The full SSN, because the masking policy evaluates the current user's role at query time.
The masked value `'***-**-****'`, because views execute with the view owner's privileges, not the querying user's role.
An error, because masking policies cannot be applied to columns referenced in views.
NULL, because the ANALYST who created the view does not have the PII_VIEWER role.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake dynamic data masking policies evaluate the CURRENT_ROLE() (or session context) of the user executing the query at runtime — not the role of the view creator. So when a `PII_VIEWER` queries the view, Snowflake evaluates the masking policy against the `PII_VIEWER` role and returns the full SSN. When an `ANALYST` queries the same view, they see the masked value. This is the key behavior of dynamic data masking — it is evaluated dynamically per query, not at view creation time.

**B** is wrong because masking policy evaluation uses the querying user's role, not the view owner's role.

**C** is wrong because masking policies work through views — the policy is on the underlying column and applies regardless of access path.

**D** is wrong because NULL is not returned; the policy decides between full value and masked value.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer runs `EXPLAIN USING TABULAR SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 12345`. They see a TableScan node scanning all 10,000 micro-partitions. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?

options.text: The query uses SELECT * which disables partition pruning.
The EXPLAIN command itself prevents pruning from being evaluated.
The customer_id column has poor clustering — its values are spread across all micro-partitions.
The orders table has too few micro-partitions to benefit from pruning.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Snowflake prunes micro-partitions using column min/max metadata. If customer_id values are randomly distributed across all micro-partitions (i.e., the min/max range of every partition includes 12345), no partitions can be eliminated and a full scan occurs.

**A** is wrong because SELECT * does not disable partition pruning — the WHERE clause drives pruning regardless of the SELECT list.

**B** is wrong because EXPLAIN evaluates the query plan including pruning estimates.

**D** is wrong because having 10,000 micro-partitions is a large table, not too few — and poor clustering on customer_id is the likely culprit.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform data provider creates a share and adds a table to it. The consumer mounts the share and queries the table. When does the consumer see new data inserted by the provider into the shared table?

options.text: Only after the provider manually refreshes the share.
After a 24-hour synchronization window.
Immediately — Snowflake data sharing is live and the consumer always queries the provider's current data.
Only when the consumer recreates their imported database from the share.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Snowflake data sharing uses a zero-copy, live model. The consumer's imported database is a logical view over the provider's storage — no data is physically copied. Any changes the provider makes (INSERTs, UPDATEs, DELETEs) are immediately visible to the consumer in their next query.

**A** is incorrect because there is no manual refresh mechanism — data sharing is always live.

**B** is incorrect because there is no synchronization delay.

**D** is incorrect because the imported database does not need to be recreated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following objects can be cloned using the Snowflake platform's zero-copy clone feature? (Select TWO)

options.text: Database
Network policy
Schema
Resource monitor

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. Snowflake supports zero-copy cloning of databases, schemas, tables, stages, file formats, sequences, streams, tasks, and pipes. Cloning a database or schema creates a deep clone of all contained objects.

**B** is wrong because network policies are account-level configuration objects and cannot be cloned.

**D** is wrong because resource monitors are account-level objects and do not support cloning.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/object-clone

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform administrator wants to understand why a specific query was slow. Which Snowflake feature provides a visual breakdown of execution nodes, time spent at each step, and data flow within the query?

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
Query Profile in Snowsight
EXPLAIN PLAN output
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Query Profile in Snowsight (the Snowflake web UI) provides an interactive, visual execution graph showing each operation node in the query plan, the time each node took, bytes scanned, rows produced, and spill information. It is the primary tool for diagnosing slow queries.

**A** is wrong because QUERY_HISTORY provides overall query metadata (total duration, credits, status) but not node-level execution detail.

**C** is wrong because EXPLAIN PLAN shows the estimated execution plan before running but does not show actual runtime statistics.

**D** is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES shows table metadata, not query execution details.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ui-query-profile

questions.stem: One team wants to unload a Snowflake query result to an Amazon S3 bucket in Parquet format. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: EXPORT TABLE my_table TO @my_s3_stage FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET);
COPY INTO @my_s3_stage FROM (SELECT * FROM my_table) FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET);
PUT @my_s3_stage SELECT * FROM my_table FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET);
UNLOAD SELECT * FROM my_table INTO @my_s3_stage FORMAT PARQUET;

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because COPY INTO supports data unloading from a table or query to a stage (internal or external). The syntax `COPY INTO @stage FROM (SELECT ...)` unloads the query result to the specified stage location. Adding FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET) specifies the output format.

**A** is wrong because EXPORT TABLE is not a Snowflake command.

**C** is wrong because PUT is for uploading local files to a stage, not for unloading query results.

**D** is wrong because UNLOAD is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer creates a table: `CREATE TABLE events (id INT, raw VARIANT)`. They load JSON data with STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE. Later they run: `SELECT TYPEOF(raw:amount) FROM events`. Some rows return 'REAL', some return 'INTEGER', and some return 'NULL_VALUE'. What explains this?

options.text: Snowflake requires all VARIANT keys to have the same type; the mixed types indicate a load error.
VARIANT columns are schema-less — different rows can have different types for the same key path, and TYPEOF() reflects the actual stored type per row.
TYPEOF() returns 'NULL_VALUE' only for rows where the raw column itself is NULL.
REAL and INTEGER both mean numeric; the difference is a display artifact with no functional significance.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because VARIANT is schema-less in Snowflake. Different rows can store different JSON types for the same key path — some rows may have `amount` as a decimal (stored as REAL), others as an integer (stored as INTEGER), and others may be missing the key entirely (TYPEOF returns 'NULL_VALUE' when the key is absent or the value is JSON null). TYPEOF() reports the actual type of the value in each row's VARIANT.

**A** is wrong because VARIANT explicitly allows mixed types — it is a feature, not an error.

**C** is wrong because 'NULL_VALUE' specifically refers to a JSON null value or missing key, not a NULL VARIANT column.

**D** is wrong because REAL and INTEGER are distinct type representations that can affect arithmetic and comparisons.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A the Snowflake platform administrator executes: `ALTER USER analyst1 SET NETWORK_POLICY = 'corp_policy';`. What is the effect?

options.text: The network policy applies to all users in the account.
The command fails because network policies can only be set at the account level.
The analyst1 user is removed from the account-level network policy and has no restrictions.
The network policy overrides the account-level network policy for analyst1 only.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because a user-level network policy overrides the account-level network policy for that specific user. This allows more granular control — for example, allowing a service account to connect from a different IP range than the corporate policy allows.

**A** is wrong because setting a policy on a specific user does not affect all users.

**B** is wrong because network policies CAN be set at both the account level and the individual user level.

**C** is wrong because the user's network policy still applies the IP restrictions defined in 'corp_policy' — they are not unrestricted.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: An application developer uses `TRY_CAST(data:price::STRING AS NUMBER)` on a VARIANT column where some rows have `price` as a string like `"N/A"`. What is returned for those rows?

options.text: The original string value 'N/A' is returned unchanged.
0 is returned as a default numeric value for failed casts.
An error is raised for each row where the cast fails.
NULL is returned for rows where the cast to NUMBER fails.

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because TRY_CAST is the safe casting function in Snowflake that returns NULL instead of raising an error when a type conversion fails. For rows where `data:price` is 'N/A' (which cannot be converted to a NUMBER), TRY_CAST returns NULL. 

**A** is wrong because TRY_CAST specifically avoids raising errors — that is the purpose of the TRY_ prefix (use CAST or :: for error-raising behavior). 

**C** is wrong because TRY_CAST returns NULL, not 0, on failure. 

**B** is wrong because TRY_CAST always returns the target type or NULL — it never returns the original value in the original type.

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer creates a Dynamic Table with `TARGET_LAG = '5 minutes'`. Which statement BEST describes this setting?

options.text: Snowflake will refresh the dynamic table exactly every 5 minutes using a fixed cron schedule.
Snowflake guarantees the dynamic table data will never be more than 5 minutes behind its base tables.
Snowflake will attempt to refresh the dynamic table so that it lags its base tables by no more than 5 minutes, on a best-effort basis.
The dynamic table will only refresh when data older than 5 minutes is detected in the base tables.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because TARGET_LAG specifies the target (not guaranteed) maximum staleness of the dynamic table relative to its base tables. Snowflake schedules refreshes to meet this target on a best-effort basis.

**A** is wrong because TARGET_LAG is not a fixed cron schedule — it is a staleness target that drives Snowflake's automated refresh scheduling.

**B** is wrong because the guarantee framing is too strong; it is a target, not a hard SLA.

**D** is wrong because the refresh is not triggered by data age within the base tables beyond the lag window.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/dynamic-tables-intro

questions.stem: Which TWO file formats does the Snowflake platform support natively for loading semi-structured data? (Select TWO)

options.text: PARQUET
AVRO
XLSX (Microsoft Excel)
DOCX (Microsoft Word)

questions.explanation:
**A** and **B** are correct. Snowflake natively supports loading data from CSV, JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML file formats using COPY INTO. Parquet and Avro are commonly used semi-structured formats with native support.

**C** is wrong because Snowflake does not natively support loading XLSX files.

**D** is wrong because DOCX is a document format and is not supported for data loading.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/semistructured-intro

questions.stem: Which TWO file formats are supported natively by the Snowflake platform's COPY INTO command for loading semi-structured data? (Select TWO)

options.text: JSON
DELTA (Delta Lake)
PARQUET
ARROW (Apache Arrow)

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct because Snowflake's COPY INTO command natively supports JSON, Parquet, Avro, ORC, and XML as semi-structured data file formats, in addition to CSV/TSV for structured data. Both JSON and Parquet are explicitly listed as supported file format types.

**B** is wrong because Delta Lake format (Delta) is not a native COPY INTO file format in Snowflake — you would access Delta tables via external tables or Iceberg table integrations.

**D** is wrong because Apache Arrow is an in-memory data format used for inter-process communication and is not a file format supported by COPY INTO.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/semistructured-intro

questions.stem: An engineer creates a serverless task using `USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE = 'MEDIUM'`. How is compute billed for this task?

options.text: A Medium virtual warehouse is created, billed at 4 credits/hr for the duration of the task.
